speaker: interviewee
text: --sit up? That's why I don't want to go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Help me for a second. Can we take the picture in this-- see that paper picture next to the clock--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's her grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I don't see it. It isn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the cattle cars. And there were about 130 people with you, you said. And people were getting sick. Go on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sick because--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with what you were saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: --no oxygen, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people died there. They were thrown out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who throw them out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- either the German or the Hungarian because the Hungarian were also there. So I don't know it was Hungarian soldiers or, or the, the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you came to Auschwitz, what was the first thing you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We, we smelled something terrible-- chimney smoke. We didn't know what it is. We were frightening. Some people were very optimistic. Some people were very pessimistic. We had to go in line. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one line-- Mengele was there. And he was smiling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was not angry or hateful. It's, it's, it's unbelievable. And he, he himself killed a million, and a million people with smile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe him to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember he was a handsome man, maybe around 40, or early 40s. And smiling, said, this way, this way, this. And when came to my mother-- turn, he begged him-- she, I'm sorry, she begged him. She said, please, let me to go with my children. I'm young. I can-- I can work. I know how to work. I can do any work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, let me go with, with them. I'm young, please. And he just showed. He didn't hit her. But he showed and asked the other side. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she just-- he just forced him-- forced her to go the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this time, I went with my sister. And it was a very terrible feeling without my mother the first time. I didn't know where I'm going to see her. Where is he going to be-- where is she going to be? I'm going to see her ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had a feeling, or at least I was hoping, I'm going to see her. Otherwise, I don't think I could-- I could go through the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you about 14 years old--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they took us to a place where was no bed, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a small room, lots of people there. We, we are-- we were sleeping, if anybody could sleep, on the floor. Because on the floor was like a-- you know what they put on the roof, those paper? It's very hard. I don't know how you call that, this paper. It's like a sandpaper, but much thicker. And that's what we had to sleep. We had to take off everything, naked, and sleep, sleep on, on those paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, was it winter? Was it summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, it's May. It's, it's still, still spring. I'm sorry, June 1-- I think June 1. It's still, still spring, because June 21 is, I think, in summer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's what-- we were in three days. But the first day when we get up, we went to another barrack with my sister to look for any relatives or any friends, because we didn't come with the same people from Miskolc because we were in a-- I did mention a labor camp. And in meantime, those people were already taken. When we come to labor camp, there were from other cities. So in our city was just a few. Our barrack was a very few from Miskolc.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to another barrack, which heard-- it's 500 girls. It's all from Miskolc. We went there. And we found a-- one of my first cousin and my sister-- best girlfriend. And they said to us, you know what? You can change with somebody. You go back, tell your friends whom you are with-- tell your friends you're coming back here. And from here, somebody will go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They won't know the difference because you are not numbered. You have no name. And we agreed with that. At least we have my cousin and, and a girlfriend. And this cousin had four sister and brother all died. She was alone. And she said, I want anyway to talk to you what's going on here. Come back. And we said, OK. We went back to the barrack. In a few minute, we were telling to our friend, we are going to go to the other barrack. And we want to say goodbye to you because there is a girlfriend there of my sister and my-- and our cousin. And as soon we finish the conversation out of there, we had to go Zahlappell. And when we were in Zahlappell, we found out this barrack had to go somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: The barrack that your cousin was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and everybody died there. One person we met after the concentration camp-- from 500 people, one person lived through. And her, her legs was full of granite-- full of granite-- not a clear place. These were all black. She said everybody died. She says, I am the only one. Some people were frozen to dead. I don't know what kind barrack-- what kind camp they went-- everybody. So God was, again, with us because, because if we were to go there, we would die also.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, at first time, 10 days. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do the 10 days you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not working 10 days. Most of the time, we were in-- on Zahlappell, which mean you get very early up. You were in the heat. You were in the cold. You were in the rain-- out. And you get, let's say, a cup of something they call soup. And the people from you-- or let's say I'm the first one. They put me always in the back because they tried to hide me because I was the youngest. Who is the first one, they get the cup of so-called soup. And this one cup, everybody had to have this one cup because they didn't have any more. So I don't know, five people or 10 people-- I think five people were-- so for five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: If somebody was sick, what they can do? You are so hungry. You have to drink it. And I remember one of-- front of me, this woman, she had all kind of [MOAN] open wound all over, on the mouth, and everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, I would say I'd rather die than drink it. But I was desperate. I drink after her, and the other people after, and the other people. It was awful. So this was 10 days. And 10 days, again, Zahlappell went every day. And one day, they said, again, Zahlappell. This was in daytime. In the evening, started to train terrible. First, was very hard. The evening was so rainy. Everything was soaking wet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, we are going to take you somewhere. We went to a cattle car. And my sister said, what's going to happen to us? We were freezing because of cold and the rain. And we didn't change. They did not give us dry clothes. So we went in the train. And we were crying and crying. And we were waiting in the train, and-- I think five, six hours waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to Plaszow. Plaszow was next to Cracow in Poland. And we were there six weeks. We were working there-- hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like a mountain, or, or something, to straighten down because they wanted to build something. And I don't know how you call that [NON-ENGLISH]. It's like you had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: A pick. You had to break the rocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The heavy, heavy one, that's what we had to work with, yes. And if somebody couldn't, they either beaten to dead or they killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were many times beaten. I, I remember one time before they took us to Plaszow, one woman-- I think she was completely out of her mind. I was in the first one this time. And whoever-- anybody who was standing in the first line, he give a slap. But this slap was so hard, right away I fell down, you know. We were getting a lots of beating, a lots of beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was doing the beating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of time, the women SS, yeah, and-- or the men SS, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad, like anywhere else, very bad. And saw lots of people to be killed there also. And food was-- very little food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Plaszow-- over there, I remember one time-- couple things-- two or three things happened. One time, then they-- in the mountain, or the hill, we saw some people. They brought in, I think, politician, have-- underground people, OK. They go to the underground? OK. They were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were beaten, some to dead. And same-- some were shoot-- shot. And one man, I remember, was on, on his knees and hand. And he already had a, a, a bullet on his head somewhere. They want-- to get up. And he couldn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when he didn't get up, they were thinking to shot another one or not. And they left him suffer. They did not shot him again. And the same night, they were the next barrack to us. It was terrible. People were screaming. And we found out the next day, some people were-- the, the ear they were cutting off, and things like that. They were torturing them to dead. And another day, again, they brought in people like that over there in Plaszow. And they had to put everything down. They had a little salt, a little sugar, maybe a piece of bread, not much. But they took us-- took everything away from them. They had to leave it there. And I was working in a hill. This was a different hill than I told before. This was the same-- in Plaszow, but different place. And when I saw they left that there, they are throwing it out. And they took them away, the, the people, the underground people. I went down. And I steal those things. I was-- and maybe another person or two. Because they were afraid because they were shooting us. And my sister was terrible afraid-- terrible afraid. She said, Shari, they are going to kill you. I said, but we have to eat. So I went down. I brought up something for her and for me. And I was very lucky. God was again with me. I was not killed. And at least we had something to eat, not much, but something. But I take a lot-- lots of time chances to, to survive. In the same place-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] same place was-- I stole-- on a different day-- stole cabbage from the wagon from the German. They brought in cabbage. And they did-- they did not see me. I went there and took two cabbage. I put it in here. I had a string. I put a string here. And I walked, a little girl with two big, like, bosom or something. So, again, we ate something. People, again, were shooting at me. I was running zig and zag-- zigzag. And, again, you know, my sister and I were saved. And I shared with some people whom I was with because everybody was hungry. So everybody get a little something from the cabbage. And one more things happen which I don't forget. Also in, in the same hill, there were-- lots of kapo had prisoners who worked there who rushed us to work. And one day, they were very upset. They are always upset. We are upset. But one day was very upset because they knew some SS coming up there to look around. And one girl was there. And this was quite-- we were not too long from home yet. So she was in good condition yet. And she was pregnant, too. And so she was married. But she was young. And when the SS-- SS come, let the German shepherd go, and bitten her breast off, one of the breast, just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: For no reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: For no reason. This girl was in agony. We were all agony, first for our life, and to see somebody-- what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know. We don't know. She was doing the work like everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks. And then from there, they were close, the-- [SIGHS] I think the American or Russian, I don't know. They took us back to Auschwitz, go through again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you back to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German. They took us back to Auschwitz again-- no food, no water. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still with your sister at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh yes. If my sister-- if I would not survive, my sister would not survive. If she would not survive, I would not survive. We-- somebody had to be stronger. And I was the younger, younger person. But she was a very-- delicate. And I was, from womb, a little bit rougher than she was because I was with my mother, help her to do things, to pick up things when she went to school, and did other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I was just as afraid as she was, but I didn't show it. Because if-- I said to myself, if I'm going to show it, we both going to die. And we hoped to, to see my mother again, and my brother again, and my grandfather again. It was a dream. What happened when you got back to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We went back to Auschwitz. We were there four weeks. We get tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what your number is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, A17836. I don't have to look at. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it was like getting the tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You are standing in line. Everybody's going tattoo who-- whom they are going to send to work. And one of the German, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they came and tattooed. It look like a pen, OK. And it's like a little dot. Each, each number is a dot, you know. I don't remember-- just for a second, I put my glasses on. I am going to see how many-- maybe I can see it. I cannot see it. It's, it's probably 10 dot, or 20 dot, the A, you know, little-- it's like, somebody take a needle and put it in your arm. So it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they use the same one over and over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, they did. They did not change anything, no. And the same time, they-- I think the same time, we get the card to fill it out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --how good we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a postcard?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a postcard. And they, they collect it. And we knew we had to say right thing, or good things, because otherwise we would be all killed. So-- and they even told us what to say. You know, we are in good hands. We are eating well. We have a nice place to say. And, and we are going to go home soon, and all kind of things-- but postcard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did it actually get sent? Do you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They was sending it to Hungary, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you send yours to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my aunt in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if she ever got it?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I understood, they did. That's why they were hoping, you know, everybody's OK, yeah. At this time, she was alive yet, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the tattoos, where did you go? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the tattoos, we were still staying in, in Auschwitz. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: --for-- no, we are not working. But they did always some kind of work for us. We are not sitting, always something. My, my sister get very, very sick. And, again, I run to the kitchen. I saw a pitcher not too far. And over there, to go one steps, we thought the SS to see you. Otherwise they kill you. But I didn't say anything to my sister. I said to myself, if I'm not going to go to the kitchen, bring her something, it's not going to survive. And otherwise, I don't want to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the kitchen. And I grabbed-- in a little bowl was farina. And the girls who were working there are Jewish girls, I think, from Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, they are going to kill you. Get out of here. They are going to kill you-- was all over, SS. I, I grab the bowl. I went back to my sister. What happen to my sister, she had diarrhea. And everybody who had diarrhea-- so far, everybody, died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I took-- I found her in the latrine. You know what is latrine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were sitting in latrine-- is men and women next to each other. And there was two side-- men, women, men, whichever, you know? And right behind them-- again, it was not separate for women and men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she came out. And I said, I brought you something. You have to eat. She said, I can't eat. I'm so sick. I can't eat. I have diarrhea. I said, this will help you. This will help you. You have to. You have to get well. We are going to see our mother and grandfather. We'll get out of here. She ate it. And somehow, God helped her and helped me. She get better. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after four weeks, they took us to Augsburg-- no, excuse me. We were with a couple people whom we knew from Hungary. In, in Auschwitz, in the barracks where you were, where there are other girls there from your hometown at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the-- Auschwitz, from the beginning were two sisters who lived in Miskolc. They are not from the same city. But they moved there and rented from my mother one room. We had two rooms. And one room-- and we were-- they-- one room, four of us. And other room, we rented for. And they were very nice girls, and older than we were. They were in their 20s. And my mother said, [? Doris-- ?] this was one of the girl name-- if something happen to, to me, please, take care of our-- take care of my children. Take care of them. And she was very nice, very good. We were together. But one day in Auschwitz, they said, we need 500 girls. So they counting people and-- in one room, or one-- I don't know, it was barrack or whatever-- 500 people. And they were counted over there. In meantime, [SIGHS] my sister said, they are going-- we are going to be all alone, Shari. We were in Plaszow together, in Auschwitz together. And no, we have to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, OK, we are going to try to go closer to count us. What happened? My sister went to the other side where they were. I said myself, my God-- and they closed up. Here I am alone. She's over there. What's going to happen? They don't let me go over there. And nobody can come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few minutes, everybody waiting, few hundred people were this side. 500 people were this side. And all of a sudden, somebody from the back, a girl, a SS, hold on her, and throw her back.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was my sister. It was 501-- and needed 500. Isn't it God did it? I believe very firmly this was God. God, God helped us. What happened to those 500 girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them died. My, my-- this friend whom was taking care of us, or was supposed to taking care of us, she went-- she lost her mind. And then she died. And some people-- of her-- one of her sister is still alive. But she was-- this I don't understand. If I run by a doctor-- would explain to me-- maybe you can explain to me-- how it happen. She was about 6 foot 2. I saw her a few years here in Los Angeles, came to visit a brother. She's not, not taller than I am. I couldn't recognize her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know what happened. They said, in concentration camp. I don't know what they done to her. I never ask. But she became a short from a very tall girl. And most of the people over there in-- also all died, tortured. What kind of girls were you in the barracks with? Girls from different countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most of them were Hungary-- Hungarian over there, yes, Hungarian were. And those were-- yeah. We met a few Italian people. But wherever we went, we were always with Hungarians. I don't know how-- it's the barrack, you know, how they were. It was Hungarian, and-- Hungarian Jews, I should say. And so she came back to our side. So we were hugged. And we are going to be alone. I said, no, we are together. And, you see, if you would be there, then neither of us would survive. And we don't know where they're going. We don't know what, what happen to us. But anyway, we were there yet. And one day, again, Zahlappell. And they took us to Augsburg. We didn't know where they taking us. We didn't know the month. We didn't know the day. We don't know the date. We didn't-- we didn't know where we are. We don't know where we're going. We don't know what time it is, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went, again, with the cattle car. And, again, they put as many people as they could. 500 people went to, to Augsburg. And we were in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: For 500 people were two rooms. And we were all working. The one room-- in one room that was there, they were working for the same, same factory, but a different location. And we were working also-- I think Messerschmitt. We had a little tag, KUKA Factory. That's what it said in Augsburg. We walked. It was cold. We were there seven months, as I understood. And we-- our shoes was not the best shoes. And sometimes, it was snowing. And that's when I think I got my sickness, when I became sick. And I was sick or not, I had to go to work. We had a doctor. The doctor was a medical student, really. That what-- they made out of her a doctor. And we were-- we were working in-- over there. And one day-- one, one night, first of all when we get there-- maybe a couple nights after. We were still on the floor. And I get up. We were sleeping. That's my sister said. We were sleeping. But I thought I was up. And I heard a voice. Sarele-- that's my name, my Jewish name-- Sarele, come with me. Come with me. Sara, come with me, come. I said, grandma, grandfather, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm coming. I'm coming. I get up. And I started to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my sister said, where are you going? He was-- she was sleeping. I said, I'm going with grandfather. He's here. He's asking me to go. She said, nobody's here. Very, very dark-- very dark, was no light, nothing. And then I lay down again. And I could swear he was there. And what does it mean? Maybe someday I will ask a, a rabbi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know--
